data.title: U.S. Earnings: Broadening Strength
data.description:
Transcript

We just had three years of double-digit returns for S&P 500 that is quite rare, and if we make it into four depends a lot on earnings. Q4 is starting to report, and there are three themes on my mind.

1) Catching up

The first theme is some broadening out, ie. the gap between recent earnings outperformers and the rest of the market is shrinking somewhat. And here I'm referring to Mag 7 and S&P 493, but also U.S. market have been leading in earnings delivery and rest of the world.

2) Mega forces support cyclical sectors

The second theme is mega forces supporting some cyclical sectors. BlackRock rolled out the framing of mega forces almost four years ago, and they have been quite helpful in understanding the markets and the economy.

If we look at AI, which is one mega force, what that means for hardware and building construction and energy. If we look at energy transition, which is another mega force, what that means for grid upgrades and renewable projects.

If we look at geopolitical fragmentation, that is another mega force, what that means for defense spending and defense infrastructure. And when we bring all of them together, there are sectors, some cyclical sectors like industrials, like material industrial metals, that sit at the intersection and that is quite powerful.

3) Productivity boost

The third theme is watching productivity boost appearing, starting to appear in some sectors, and here the pattern for earnings that we should watch out for through the course of the year is that we start the year strong but through the course of the year, analysts then guided downwards bit by bit. That pattern may still repeat, but offsetting that is - partially offsetting that - may be productivity boost coming through because of AI, and here some sectors that we watch out for tech is, of course, communications services, but also healthcare. Our fundamental equity global CIO Carrie King calls healthcare the sleeper sector; many names are heavily discounted versus historical averages, and at the same time, 80% of those companies are guiding upwards, so a powerful combination.

So, the big picture is that yes, we start 2026 risk-on supported by robust earnings, supported by good growth backdrop and also central banks, in particular the Fed, poised to cut further. But underneath the hood, there are sub-themes that are playing out that are really, really interesting that allow us to be active and selective.

____________

Solid U.S. economic growth and Federal Reserve rate cuts have boosted corporate earnings and profit margins, lifting U.S. stocks and underpinning our overweight. We think this will keep playing out in Q4 earnings results starting this week. We see three themes: a further narrowing of the earnings gap between the “Magnificent 7” stocks and other sectors; mega forces supporting cyclical sectors; and AI-led productivity gains potentially offsetting typical earnings downgrades.

Catching up

Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.
Note: The bars show calendar year change in earnings for the “Magnificent 7” stocks (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, Tesla) and the rest of S&P 500 companies. Shaded bars show analyst forecasts.

The U.S. corporate earnings season is key after the S&P 500 posted a third straight year of double-digit returns in 2025, while international markets from Spain to South Korea also delivered strong results. The AI buildout and easing tariff concerns kept economic growth resilient, helping U.S. earnings beat expectations in the third quarter, as we expected. We think earnings can keep delivering, partly as the U.S. stocks driving earnings growth broaden out. The gap between the Magnificent 7 mega-cap stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) and the rest of the S&P 500 is narrowing as the other 493 see earnings improve - the first theme we’re watching. See the chart above. Yet, the Magnificent 7 are still delivering strong earnings growth - and have consistently beat expectations in recent years, according to Bloomberg data, so that gap may not narrow as much as the consensus implies.

We still prefer tech broadly, as earnings growth looks healthy and poised to broaden, both within the U.S. and globally. S&P earnings and profit margins have also proved more resilient to tariffs than many investors expected. Consensus expectations for the Magnificent 7 have been revised upward to show 20% earnings growth in the fourth quarter versus a year ago and then holding up at 19% in 2026, according to Bloomberg data. That compares with 6% for the other S&P 493 in the fourth quarter and 15% in 2026. Such earnings strength is why U.S. tech stocks depended less on investors pricing in higher valuations for gains last year relative to Europe and other regions. From the U.S. “reciprocal tariff” lows in April, the MSCI USA slightly outperformed the MSCI index of global stocks excluding the U.S. in 2025 and outperformed the same index in local currency terms by six percentage points, according to LSEG data.

Powerful mega forces can trump the macro

Second, mega forces and lower Fed policy rates are helping boost cyclical sectors linked to stronger growth, like industrials and materials. This reinforces how we are not in a typical business cycle and mega forces are trumping the traditional macro in driving returns - one of our 2026 Global Outlook themes. Sectors including industrials and materials sit at the intersection of these mega forces: the construction and energy required in the AI data center buildout; the power grid upgrades and infrastructure investment in the energy transition; and increased defense spending tied to geopolitical fragmentation.

Our third theme: Potential productivity gains from AI could break the usual pattern of earnings estimates typically starting high and being revised down as the year progresses. We like financials in both the U.S. and Europe, with the U.S. benefitting from stronger dealmaking activity and lighter regulation. We find that financials is one of the sectors talking the most about AI productivity benefits in earnings calls. The healthcare sector is a laggard that we think is ripe for potential productivity improvements and innovation, with 80% of U.S. healthcare companies guiding earnings expectations higher, FactSet data show. We’re closely watching earnings for evidence of AI-related productivity gains and new profit pools forming.

Our bottom line

We stay overweight U.S. equities and pro-risk on the AI theme. We eye opportunities in sectors beyond tech, like healthcare, that benefit from AI innovation and see mega forces supporting some key cyclical sectors like industrials.

Market backdrop

The S&P 500 advanced nearly 2% to a fresh record high in the first full trading week of 2026 while European stocks outperformed. The U.S. December jobs report reinforced our view of a “no hiring, no firing” stasis in the labor market. Yet, renewed wage gains could point to sticky inflation that will curb how soon the Fed might cut rates again. U.S. 10-year Treasury yields stayed in a tight range around 4.15%. Gold jumped more than 4% back to near-record highs.

Attention turns to the U.S. CPI after recent U.S. data proved noisy, owing to disruptions from the government shutdown. November U.S. CPI reflected partially collected data, and no data was collected for October - making this week’s December CPI report and the following releases clearer signals for assessing inflation. Early-year CPI has been strong in recent years, so inflation could surprise to the upside and curb expectations for Fed rate cuts.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Waste Management: Buy This Trash Play With Robust Revenue Growth And Consistent Capital Return
data.description:
Back in late October of last year, I made the decision to take a fresh look at Waste Management (WM). This is the kind of company that I find to be intriguing. It is conceptually a simple business, and it is required in order for society to operate at the scale that it does. The US has somewhere around 340 million people in it, and we produce a lot of trash. As population grows, there is the opportunity for additional revenue and profit growth for the company. And in almost any economic environment, it should hold up quite well, though there could be some variation in some parts of this space. In my last article about the company, I decided to reaffirm the business as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was in spite of the fact that the company had underperformed the market since my previous article about it in March. But at the end of the day, I viewed this as a high-quality prospect that would do well long-term. Since then, it has outperformed the market, rising 2.5%. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 1.5%.

When looking at the latest developments from the company, one thing I noticed was that management is becoming more focused on returning capital to shareholders. Although I always prefer growth, the fact that the company is healthy enough to return large amounts of cash to investors is great in and of itself. So in addition to tackling this article from the perspective of whether or not the company is healthy and attractively priced, I also wanted to touch on some of this. What I found was that, while the company has been inconsistent when it comes to buying back stock, it has been very consistent when paying out distributions. And with the trajectory the company is currently on, we can probably expect more of this, as well as some nice buyback activity.

Even though I am bullish about the company, there is always the possibility that fundamental conditions could worsen. I think the likelihood of that is incredibly small. But it's not impossible. The good news is that new data is coming out soon, with management expected to announce financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on January 28th. Leading up to that point, we should expect revenue, profits, and likely cash flows to all grow nicely. But if something big and unexpected occurs, a downgrade would not be unthinkable.

A Great Business at an Okay Price

Focusing first on the fundamentals, I can say that Waste Management is undeniably a healthy business. Take data covering the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year as an example. This is the most recent quarter for which we have data available. During that time, revenue amounted to $6.44 billion. That happens to be 14.9% above the $5.61 billion the company reported a year earlier. This rise in revenue is rather substantial for a company that is in such a mature and slow-growing space. So this does deserve some unpacking.

When looking at the data, the first thing that we can see is that most of the revenue growth came from the company’s WM Healthcare Solutions business. On a net basis, revenue was $628 million for the quarter. That compares to a big old goose egg for the same quarter the previous year. This stems from the fact that, in November of 2024, the company completed its acquisition of Stericycle, a rather significant player in the waste and compliance services and secure information destruction services space. This was an acquisition that cost shareholders about $7.2 billion on an enterprise value basis. The revenue associated with WM Healthcare Solutions comes specifically from that purchase.

This is not the only growth that the company exhibited. Most notably, collection revenue for the business grew from $3.80 billion to $3.93 billion year over year. The company benefited here from a growth in the average yield, which essentially refers to higher prices that it was able to charge its customers. This is a common theme in this industry. The fact of the matter is that these companies slowly but surely ratchet up how much they charge customers. And even though it wouldn't be appropriate to say that this is a monopoly, I guess you could say that the operational moat that firms like Waste Management accomplish through contracts and acquisitions over time puts them in a favorable position to hike prices.

There were other contributors to expansion as well. But they weren't as meaningful. For instance, landfill revenue expanded from $923 million to $995 million. And transfer revenue expanded from $365 million to $396 million. The overall growth of the company can be attributed in part to acquisition activities. I already covered the purchase of Stericycle. But also during the first nine months of 2025, the company completed solid waste and recycling acquisitions amounting to $442 million. Obviously, rolling new businesses into the mix is an easy way to expand.

Even though the company enjoyed a nice increase in revenue year over year, profitability did face some issues. Net income contracted from $760 million to $603 million. But this was primarily because of a $207 million increase in losses from asset sales, impairments, and other one-time items. Without this, net profits would have been higher. And this shows up rather convincingly in the form of increased cash flows. Operating cash flow, as an example, jumped from $1.36 billion to $1.59 billion. On an adjusted basis where we strip out changes in working capital, we would get growth from $1.42 billion to $1.80 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.69 billion to $1.95 billion.

In the chart above, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. This further demonstrates the consistent and reliable growth that the company has achieved. Even though there was a decline in overall net profits, the fact that revenue and cash flows all grew without exception is encouraging. What's more, management expects this trend to continue.

They believe that revenue for 2025 will be around $25.275 billion. That compares favorably to the $22.06 billion the company reported in 2024. And with this growth in the top line, we should get EBITDA of somewhere between $7.475 billion and $7.625 billion. The midpoint thereof, $7.55 billion, is meaningfully higher than the $6.56 billion the company reported for 2024. Assuming that other profitability metrics continue to scale as they have throughout the year, we would anticipate net profits of about $2.51 billion and adjusted operating cash flow of $6.48 billion.

The picture for the company is so positive that management has decided to ratchet up how much capital it's returning to shareholders. In the chart above, you can see its history over the last few years of paying out dividends and buying back stock. Although in prior years share buybacks were rather significant, they scaled back considerably because of their large purchase of Stericycle. In fact, in the chart below, you can see how net debt and the net leverage ratio for the company changed over time. It popped from 2.67 in 2023 to 3.58 in 2024. And management's objective was to lower this, with a target ultimately of between 2.5 and 3. The good news is that, using projected results for the 2025 fiscal year and the net debt that the company had at the end of the third quarter of the year, the net leverage ratio is already down to 3.07. Most likely, it will be down even further when management announces results for the final quarter of 2025. But even if it doesn't drop further, it's lower than two of the three firms similar to it that I included in that chart.

The progress that management has made gave it the confidence to hike the dividend further. On December 15th, management announced that they were increasing the dividend by 14.5%. This is rather substantial, translating to a quarterly rate of $0.945 per share this year compared to the $0.825 per share last year. This actually makes for the 23rd consecutive year of dividend increases. Clearly, the company is doing something right. But that wasn't even the biggest development. The firm also announced a new $3 billion share buyback program that will supersede the authority remaining under its previous $1.5 billion buyback program that was initiated in 2023. This suggests to me that management believes that the decline in the net leverage ratio justifies further share purchases. At the same time as this, the company believes that it will make further progress on its net leverage ratio, reducing it to its long-term target of between 2.5 and 3 by the end of this year. So on the whole, things are going great.

If we use the projected cash flow figures for the 2025 fiscal year and the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of Waste Management are currently priced. Because of the purchase of Stericycle, I would argue that the 2025 valuation metrics are much more representative of the overall value proposition for shareholders today. That chart also compares the company to three other similar firms. And what we see here is that, without exception, it is cheaper than these other prospects. This is certainly appealing and suggests that some upside can be captured even from the company trading at multiples that are more reasonable for its space. I would also make the case here that the net leverage ratio justifies perhaps a premium compared to these other comparable firms, the earlier chart on that in this article demonstrating how the picture looks here.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that, after the market closes on January 28th, the management team at Waste Management will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Driven almost entirely by acquisition activity, analysts believe that revenue will come in at about $6.39 billion. That's up considerably from the $5.89 billion the company reported a year earlier. With revenue growing, profits are also expected to expand, with earnings per share anticipated to rise from $1.48 to $1.92. That would take net profits up from $598 million to $776 million. In the table below, you can also see metrics for the final quarter of 2024. In all likelihood, a rise in net profits will translate to an increase here as well.

Takeaway

Right now, I would say that things are going quite well for shareholders of Waste Management. The company is expanding nicely and seems healthy. Leverage is in check, and management is taking that opportunity to return capital to shareholders, not only in the form of dividends but through share buybacks as well. Relative to other similar firms, the company is trading on the cheap, and the net leverage ratio is near the lower end of the spectrum. When you add on top of this other growth opportunities that I covered in my previous article, such as those involving its sustainability investments that management is making, including $1.4 billion in recycling infrastructure over the next couple of years and $1.6 billion in RNG plant development, I don't see any reason to be anything other than bullish at this time.

data.title: Q1 2026 Outlook: Visibility Means Risk On
data.description:
Entering 2026, clearer fiscal and monetary signals support a more risk-on outlook, as AI, private credit, gold, India and crypto reset into more compelling opportunities.

As we move into 2026, markets are operating in an environment with something investors have not had in years: visibility. That clarity around fiscal policy, monetary direction and major investment themes supports a more constructive, risk-on posture, even as selectivity remains critical.

Fewer Fiscal and Monetary Surprises Ahead

One of the most important developments for markets is the gradual improvement in the U.S. fiscal picture. While deficits remain elevated, they are shrinking as a percentage of GDP from the historic highs reached during the COVID period. This fiscal stabilization is helping anchor longer-term interest rates and reduce tail risks.

At the same time, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has articulated a new Federal Reserve philosophy that is less activist and more restrained. His comments suggest that the upcoming Fed Chair appointment process in May should be smooth, alleviating fears of institutional disruption or politicized monetary policy.

On rates, Bessent’s characterization of current interest levels as “normal” is telling. Markets should not expect aggressive or destabilizing short-term rate cuts in 2026. Instead, the outlook points to steady policy, modest adjustments, and fewer shocks—another contributor to improved visibility.

AI Bubble Has Popped, Creating Good Setup

After a sharp selloff in selected AI-related stocks late last year, the AI trade looks more attractive today than it did at the “nosebleed” levels seen in October. Importantly, the correction has occurred even as underlying demand for compute, tokens and productivity gains remains strong.

Adjacent themes, such as nuclear power tied to AI-driven electricity demand, have also repriced meaningfully. In our view, this reset improves the risk-reward profile for investors with a medium-term horizon.

Nuclear Stocks Corrected in Q4

Source: Bloomberg. Data as of December 31, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of strategy performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

BDCs Back in Focus

Business development companies (BDCs) experienced a difficult 2025, but that correction has created opportunity. With yields remaining attractive and credit fears largely priced in, BDCs now look more compelling than they did a year ago.

The same is true for the management companies behind them, such as Ares (ARCC), which now trade at valuations that are far more reasonable relative to their long-term earnings power and track records.

Gold: A Global Monetary Asset

Gold continues to re-emerge as a leading global currency, driven by central bank demand and a world that is increasingly less dollar-centric. While gold appears somewhat extended from a technical standpoint, we view pullbacks as opportunities to add exposure. The structural case remains intact.

Gold Above Support But Demand Isn’t Going Away

Source: Bloomberg. Data as of December 31, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Opportunities in India and Crypto

Beyond U.S. markets, India remains a high-conviction, long-term opportunity, supported by structural reforms and durable growth dynamics.

In crypto, Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) traditional four-year cycle broke in 2025, complicating short-term signals. While our near-term view is mixed, we remain long-term bullish, recognizing both the volatility and the structural adoption trends at play.

Important Disclosures

Bitcoin (BTC) is a decentralized digital currency, without a central bank or single administrator, that can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without the need for intermediaries.

Please note that VanEck may offer investments products that invest in the asset class(es) or industries included herein.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned is unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third-party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

The MVIS® Global Uranium & Nuclear Energy Index (MVNLRTR) is intended to track the overall performance of companies involved in: uranium mining; the construction, engineering and maintenance of nuclear power facilities and nuclear reactors; the production of electricity from nuclear sources; or providing equipment, technology and/or services to the nuclear power industry.

For other important disclosures, please read more.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

© Van Eck Associates Corporation.

Original Post

data.title: Alphabet Is The Undeniable King Of AI
data.description:
Google is Still One of the Best AI Picks Among Majors

When I upgraded Google (GOOG) stock to "Buy" in September 2024, it was off its ATH by over 21%, and the stock was lagging most of its Mag-7 peers. Since then, I updated my bullish rating a few times, and Google turned into a strong leader of its tech group, and the stock has more than doubled since June 2025. The sentiment/narrative is still very bullish despite Google's already stretched valuation, as I can assess based on Seeking Alpha's recently published articles.

I don't think one has to be a contrarian to pick a potentially outperforming stock - the momentum is strong, and it's likely to stay such because Google's vertically integrated AI stack that competitors can't easily replicate helps them gain market share in the most lucrative niches, thus extending their total addressable markets. This process is already profitable for the firm, which is a rarity in this AI cycle. The disruption to their search business is no longer a valid risk because a) Gemini is gaining share versus OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT, and b) there are plenty of new niches that could potentially provide Google with higher growth rates than search in the future. I keep my rating with no changes, as I expect a few years of sustainably high valuation multiples.

Why Do I Think So?

I think we need to assess the catalysts that might drive Google's stock price over the next few years. One of the most significant ones is the 7th Generation TPU - Ironwood - which reportedly delivers "a 10x peak performance improvement over the TPU v5p and 4x better performance per chip". It's a huge advantage over AI hyperscaler peers like Meta (META) or Microsoft (MSFT), which invest a lot into their AI ventures but remain dependent on Nvidia's (NVDA) GPUs. Based on Morgan Stanley's research, Google can potentially save up to 88% on running its TPUs (instead of running a Blackwell cluster), although Nvidia's chips are still more energy efficient.

Take AI inference, which does not require as much computing power. Installing a cluster of 24,000 Blackwell processors means upfront hardware costs of $852 million, Morgan Stanley estimated, leading to annual depreciation charges of $213 million over four years. A same-sized cluster of Google TPUs will cost $99 million in hardware, and $25 million in annual depreciation. Developing chipmaking capabilities will also enable companies to drive a harder bargain with Nvidia.

Reuters

Having a massive cost advantage in US dollars by leveraging internal silicon, Google is driving its marginal cost of AI inference toward zero, as one of Seeking Alpha's analysts, Esxeleryn Analytics, suggested in their recent research piece.

For more clarity, in my opinion, Google’s ability to run Gemini on internal silicon can drive the marginal cost of an AI search query toward zero far faster than Bing and ChatGPT, which are shackled to commercial GPU pricing and margins. This silicon advantage creates a Compute Flywheel. First, it leads to lower inference costs, as Google can afford to infuse heavy AI models (like Gemini 3 Deep Think) into free products (AI Studio, Search, YouTube, Gmail) without destroying margins. Direct competitors may not match this without incurring losses/charging relatively higher subscription fees, in my opinion. The second thing is Data Gravity. Lower costs allow Google to process more tokens (already processing 1.3 quadrillion monthly tokens, +20x YoY) than the competitors. This massive data ingestion goes back into model optimization. As a result, it is creating a data-based moat that improves the TPUs' efficiency on a forward basis.

Source: Esxeleryn Analytics's article

With a lower cost, Google can put more resources into Gemini's development, being much more flexible on internal capital management and R&D allocation. And they can embed high-complexity models like Gemini 3 Deep Think into free products like Search and YouTube because it's not that hard to do when it's economically a few times less expensive than what the industry would pay for it. As a result, Google isn't destroying its consolidated operating margins, and it gets a rise in its market share, particularly in the AI chatbot market, by not overspending on marketing.

The other strong multi-year catalyst that I see for Google is the ongoing transition in the Agentic Commerce. If you remember, last year or so, the Street was worried about AI agents eventually eating away at Google's search share. I think this is not a concern anymore. In 2026, Google launched UCP (so-called "Universal Commerce Protocol"), which allows Gemini to act as a unified shopping interface, first in partnership with Walmart (WMT) and Sam's Club. Users can move from discovery to payment via Google Pay within a single conversational thread, so Google is effectively shifting from just selling clicks (as an ad) to taking a percentage of fulfilled transactions. It's a long-term catalyst, and I'm not suggesting that Google's business model will change tomorrow. But I like the tendency because the revenue diversification it can bring to the table seems to be massive. We'll need a few years to see the real tangible results, but patience should pay off, in my opinion.

Another spot is logistics, where Alphabet's Wing is scaling through a partnership with the same Walmart, first across 270 consumer locations, which is likely to be extended further. They're basically competing with Amazon (AMZN) in this AI-to-doorstep niche, and it's another addition to Google's hard-to-estimate TAM.

What has already scaled even further than Wing is Waymo, which is currently the leader in autonomous ride-hailing, and it's operating ~450,000 fully autonomous paid rides per week as of late 2025.

This year, they're going to launch service in London and Tokyo, plus expand in Dallas, Nashville, Seattle, and airports in San Francisco and San Jose, so we might see another doubling in rides/week.

Mawakana said Waymo plans to increase its weekly autonomous rides from the hundreds of thousands to one million by the end of 2026.

Source: Observer

Waymo alone can be worth $100 billion, according to Electrek, which is double its 2024 valuation. I think that if they scale further and achieve a million rides a week next year (or in 2027), the valuation might be close to $200-2500 billion by the end of 2026/27. It's not a big addition to Google's market cap, though, but Google might spin it off and get more capital flexibility for the firm's core initiatives.

One more catalyst should come from Google Cloud, which is seeing profitability acceleration. In Q3 2025, Cloud's EBIT margin reached 23.7%, and its total market share is roughly 13-15.6%. Even if their Q3 incremental revenue versus Q2 growth was lower than Azure's and AWS's, the backlog's QoQ expansion of 46% (~$155 billion) in the latest quarter is telling me that 2026/27 is going to bring higher growth in Cloud and likely a rise in margins. Why margins? Their $4.75 billion acquisition of Intersect should secure a pipeline of ~10.8 gigawatts of power projects by 2028, so Google is effectively decoupling from the commercial energy grid and can lower its Cloud-related COGS in the medium term. At least this is my understanding.

Now, with all these catalysts in mind, let's turn to Google's valuation.

The stock is valued only slightly above the long-term P/E average (29.54x vs. 28.88x), and the 1-year forward metric is put at only 25.56x, based on YCharts data.

Over the past 2 years, Google beat the EPS consensus by 0.7-0.9%, but the earnings revisions have changed dramatically in the past few years. For example, since late 2020, the expectation for FY2025 moved from $5.64 to $10.55 (as of mid-January 2026).

So, I think this trend will continue as we receive more updates on fresh TPU rollouts, higher margins at Cloud, and possibly less cash burn at Waymo, to name a few.

So, I'll put a 10% premium on the FY2026 EPS consensus and assume that the stock's P/E would reach 30x by the end of this year. This would give me an upside potential of about 12% to the current stock price.

30x * $11.26 * 1.1 = $371.58/share (+12% upside potential)

Source: The author's calculations

Where Can I Be Wrong?

My above valuation calculations, although they indicate a positive upside, may be insufficient for GARP investors. In other words, the upside's size may not cover the existing risks surrounding Google these days. Based on the Street's consensus, GOOG has already reached its fair share price, and it has no upside this year:

Another risk hides in the regulatory landscape, which is only getting more and more intense. Alphabet has been barred from entering exclusive distribution contracts for Google Search, Chrome, and even the Gemini app, and while Alphabet wasn't forced to sell Chrome [September 2025], I think this risk stays in.

One more thing to keep in mind is Google's execution. The larger the firm's ecosystem becomes, the harder it is to serve and develop. At some point, Google will have to spin off certain areas, risking the loss of its dominant position and existing synergies.

The Bottom Line

I know that risks are high for buying Google stock near its ATHs, especially when we put reasonable valuation inputs in the price target calculation for 2026. But to me, GOOG remains the undeniable king of AI that has the greater chances for success given its already well-built-out ecosystem and verticalization. I see a modest upside of 12% to the rest of 2026, which can change if we see more positive earnings revisions throughout the year. For 2027, the upside may be higher if the P/E multiple stays close to 30x. Based on all this, my "Buy" rating stays in place even after the stock's rally.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Global Partners: Upgrading To Strong Buy As Market Fear Creates Value
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Global Partners LP (GLP), I highlighted the company's strong yield and solid investments into its vertically integrated mid- and downstream business, calling it an underdog that's ready for the next energy upcycle.

With a solid report released in the meantime that reaffirms their resilience, the company announced its 16th consecutive quarterly distribution increase and an even better stock price following a slight decrease. I will upgrade GLP to a Strong Buy, despite the near-term headwinds stemming from the macro environment limiting its expansion potential.

Internal Developments

GLP reported an overall solid Q3, although their profitability was affected due to retail fuel margins normalizing after a strong year, while the Q3 Distributable Cash Flow reached $52.98 million (compared to $71.13 million in Q3'24) and $150.64 million during 9M'25.

Still, the company advanced on its investments, targeting $85 million to $105 million in CAPEX during 2025 as they continue to optimize their terminal network and expand marine fuel operations as well, upgrading their midstream operations to support their downstream. Of this, roughly $40 million to $50 million (excluding acquisitions) is expected to be used primarily for gasoline stations and terminal investments.

Recently, GLP announced its 16th consecutive quarterly distribution increase, with its quarterly cash distribution reaching $0.755 per common unit ($3.02 annualized), with the yield reaching ~7.08% at the moment of writing this. That's about $102 million per year, which is still a very manageable payout ratio of about 50% if we annualize their 9M'25 DCF.

Financially, based on GLP's latest report, we see a solid position overall, with current assets above their current liabilities, although I'll mention again that the value of their inventories can change in this rapidly shifting environment with oil prices on the decline.

I still believe that they should focus on reducing their debt in the future, as even though the company has recently refinanced its 2027 debt and has no senior notes due until all the way in 2029, they still have significant interest expenses to worry about, as they depend on quite expensive external capital.

To develop a bit on that, we're talking about a similar position to REITs today, where they have to pay most of what they make back to us as dividends and have little cash to actually invest, forcing them to rely either on issuing debt, issuing stock, or recycling assets if they want to acquire something. In GLP's case, we're talking about quite expensive debt (7.125% senior notes issued recently) and almost identical costs for issuing units, as they also come with a ~7.08% yield today.

Because of this, the company doesn't have that much flexibility if it wants to take advantage of the environment, as the cost of external capital is very elevated nowadays, potentially forcing them into a self-preservation mode instead.

Something else they can do to save quite a bit of money is call back their preferred stock, Global Partners LP 9.50 RED PFD B (GLP.PR.B), which has a base 9.5% yield and is callable starting on May 15th, 2026, being a very "quick and simple" way for them to save some money—much better than repurchasing common units or repaying debt. I believe the preferred is at relatively high risk of being called, while it trades at $25.83, which is ~3.21% above its $25 par value.

With the latest ex-dividend declared being on February 2, that means they'll pay at least 2 more dividends until becoming callable, which is $1.1875 in dividends or ~4.60%, meaning you would get roughly 1.4% in profit if bought today over roughly 4 months (assuming they call it immediately), for a roughly 4.2% annualized yield, which is currently around the yield paid by the theoretical "risk-free" treasuries without having to assume the same risks.

Macro Developments

Meanwhile, despite depending much less on oil prices, Venezuela, Iran, OPEC decisions, etc. than upstream companies that literally rely on that, GLP still depends significantly on the currently weak consumer environment, with the management highlighting the weakness persisting especially for lower-income consumers during their Q3 Earnings Call:

There's definitely pressure on lower-income. You see consumers trading down from more premium brands to more sub-generic brands. We continue to try and leverage our loyalty program that Eric mentioned earlier to grow promotions. But I think it's -- yes, they've definitely been under pressure overall.

As mentioned before, we have several recent studies reinforcing this idea, with the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index improving in December but still low overall, coming after November 2025 was the second-lowest level seen in the history of the survey. Other studies highlight the significant stress seen by the consumer, with pressure on the general consumer environment due to lower budgets, job insecurity, and rising costs, with little confidence going into 2026.

Meanwhile, we face rising political (and economic) uncertainty and the potential to see the market's trust in the US continue to erode. As I'm writing this, the Department of Justice opened a criminal investigation into Powell, further pushing the market away from general stocks and into safe-haven assets (including outside of the country) and weakening the US dollar.

This further ignites the market's concerns about the Fed's independence (seen even from Bank of America's CEO recently), especially with Powell's term ending in May 2026 and Trump likely naming a Fed Chair that shares his views this time.

Even though this is still 1 out of 12 votes in FOMC decisions and the market can just ignore this person if it sees that something isn't right, we're still talking about a very important factor that at least provided some clarity that can be modeled during uncertain times (Powell's remarks), and losing this can certainly affect the market at a terrible time.

Despite rate cuts being a positive, they aren't enough to lower the actual cost of debt, as the market considers other factors when deciding the long-term treasury yields it demands, influencing the SOFR and consequently the debt costs of companies like GLP, while high treasuries also make dividend stocks less attractive.

Still, these high-interest expenses, undervaluation that I'll mention below, and high dividends can also attract a potential buyer, with large players being able to not only integrate their operations and deliver synergies this way, but also renegotiate their debt at even half their rates, potentially saving very significant amounts of money and making this accretive even in the double-digit area.

Just by halving the company's interest expenses, a larger peer would already generate roughly $69 million per year, which is about 4.75% of the current market cap from the start, so I believe this possibility shouldn't be ignored.

Risks Reiterated

In short, this is a very tough environment for a company like GLP, and even though they've been through lots of uncertainty before, they still depend on the environment at the end of the day, and the market will likely ask for better risk-adjusted yields from them if the environment continues to weaken.

Meanwhile, the company is already in a position where issuing debt and equity is very expensive, being essentially forced to focus on self-preservation instead of taking advantage of potential opportunities—although I wouldn't rule that out completely.

Valuation

For GLP's valuation, I will start with a DCF of $200 million (annualizing 9M'25 and rounding down) in 2025, from which I'll discount the midpoint $45 million in expansion CAPEX they expect, for an Adjusted DCF of $155 million in 2025. I'll assume a 4% CAGR over the first 5 years despite potential for lower debt costs, expansions, and improvements in the consumer environment, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and 2.25% terminal growth rate, with a high 12% discount rate for a large margin of safety and 34.16 million units outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $1.79 billion and an intrinsic value of $52.49 per unit, which is similar to the levels from a few months ago and above the current $43.04, assuming a solid margin of safety if the company can basically stagnate around these levels with minimal growth despite all the potential improvements and investments.

Conclusions

I believe GLP is worth upgrading to a Strong Buy, offering a very solid yield and being clearly undervalued, although it faces low growth and macro pressure that will likely cause more volatility.

There is certainly room for more downside, especially if the company's operations are affected by the weaker consumer environment for longer, but the overall risk-reward definitely seems to be in their favor, and I've placed an order to buy some common units recently that's probably getting triggered by the time you're reading this.

data.title: Visible Alpha Breakdown Of U.S. Airlines' Fourth Quarter Earnings Expectations
data.description:
US airlines will kick off fourth-quarter earnings season on Tuesday, January 13, with Delta reporting first, followed by peers through to Southwest on Friday, January 30. Visible Alpha consensus estimates point to mixed earnings expectations, as steady travel demand is offset by weaker pricing, rising costs, and temporary operational disruptions linked to the recent US government shutdown.

Delta Air Lines Inc. (DAL) and United Airlines Holdings Inc. (UAL) are the first to report earnings next week. Analysts expect Delta to deliver modest revenue growth of 1.4% year-on-year, while United is projected to see a stronger growth of 4.9%, driven by higher capacity and resilient traffic. However, lower revenue per mile and rising unit costs are expected to pressure margins, with Delta’s EBITDAR expected to be down 5.1% and United’s down 3.8%.

American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL), reporting later in the month, is expected to see a 3.2% revenue growth in Q4, though profitability is likely to remain constrained.

Low-cost carrier Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV) is expected to deliver strong traffic and revenue growth, although profitability is forecast to weaken, with net income projected to fall 18%. Alaska Air Group Inc. (ALK) is forecast to see low margins, minimal net income, and heavy EPS decline, despite a modest increase in revenue. JetBlue Airways Corp. (JBLU) remains in the red, with analysts expecting another quarterly loss amid pricing pressure and capacity discipline.

Overall, as in the third quarter, profitability across airlines remains under pressure. Revenue growth is being driven largely by higher volumes rather than pricing power, while rising labor and operating costs continue to constrain margins.

Capacity and traffic:

Capacity and traffic expectations for US airlines in Q4 2025 point to continued, but measured, growth, with total available seat miles (ASM) across the group projected at 315.6 billion, up 3.7% from 304.3 billion in Q4 2024, while total revenue passenger miles (RPM) are expected to rise to 260.8 billion, up 3.4% from 252.3 billion a year earlier.

Network carriers continue to lead expansion, with United Airlines increasing capacity from 78.3 billion ASM in Q4 2024 to 82.8 billion in Q4 2025 (+5.7% YOY), alongside a rise in RPM to 68.2 billion (+5.7% YOY). Delta Air Lines shows more modest growth, with ASM rising to 73.4 billion (+1.9% YOY) and RPM to 61.2 billion (+1.3% YOY).

Among other carriers, JetBlue is projected to see slightly lower ASM (-0.5% YOY) and RPM (-0.8% YOY), while Southwest Airlines expectations seem relatively strong with ASM up 5.4% year-on-year and RPM up 5.5%, compared to last year.

Load factor remains high across the group, averaging 82.4% in Q4 2025, though slightly below the 82.7% recorded a year earlier. This reflects strong underlying demand but also suggests that capacity growth is marginally outpacing traffic, resulting in slightly less efficient seat utilization.

Revenue and pricing:

Revenue and pricing expectations for Q4 2025 point to a resilient topline but continued pressure on unit revenues. Total passenger revenue for the group is projected at $51.9 billion, up 3.6% from $50.1 billion in Q4 2024.

Legacy carriers continue to dominate revenue generation, while Southwest expectation shows the strongest growth in passenger revenue, rising to $6.8 billion (+7.9% YOY). JetBlue is the only outlier, with passenger revenues projected to decline 3.3% year-on-year to $2 billion.

Despite higher revenues, revenue per available seat mile (RASM) remains broadly flat to slightly down for most carriers due to weaker pricing power. Group-average RASM is expected to edge down marginally to 17.50¢ in Q4 2025 from 17.55¢ a year earlier.

Yield trends tell a similar story. Average yield across the group is projected at 21.22¢ in Q4 2025, broadly in line with last year, but still below mid-2024 peaks. Delta is expected to maintain the highest yield at 25.82¢, benefiting from its premium-heavy network, while analysts see United and JetBlue yield fall year-on-year.

Cost and efficiency:

Cost and efficiency metrics for Q4 2025 point to persistent margin pressure across US airlines, as rising labor costs outweigh some moderation in fuel expenses. Employee costs are rising across all carriers, with total employee costs for the group expected to increase to $19.3 billion, up 5.4% from $18.3 billion in Q4 2024, reflecting higher wages, contractual pay rises and elevated staffing levels following strong operational demand through 2025.

Overall, total expenses excluding fuel are projected to rise 5.3% to $43.9 billion in Q4 2025 from a year earlier. On a unit basis, cost per available seat mile (CASM) excluding fuel is projected to increase 2.3% year-on-year to 13.29¢, compared with 12.99¢ a year earlier.

Profitability:

Profitability across US airlines is expected to remain under pressure in Q4 2025, with net income declining for most carriers compared with Q4 2024, reflecting weaker pricing, higher costs and limited operating leverage. Aggregate net income for the group is forecast at $2.4 billion, down from $3.3 billion a year earlier.

EBITDAR trends reinforce the picture of margin compression. Meanwhile, diluted EPS is expected to moderate across the board, signaling that earnings growth is constrained by rising labor and fuel costs, as well as competitive pricing pressures.

Overall, US airlines are poised for moderate and uneven earnings. While passenger demand and travel volumes remain robust, the industry continues to grapple with higher labor costs, limited pricing power, and impact from the temporary disruption of the US government shutdown.

This article was published by Visible Alpha, part of S&P Global Market Intelligence and not by S&P Global Ratings, which is a separately managed division of S&P Global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ClearBridge Appreciation ESG Portfolios Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Benchmark performance remains highly concentrated: only three of 11 GICS sectors outperformed the index in the quarter, with technology - AI in particular - the principal driver.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in and an underweight to the health care sector and a communication services overweight proved beneficial.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the industrials, communication services, IT and consumer staples sectors detracted the most, along with a consumer staples overweight and an IT underweight.
  • Outlook: Policy remains supportive of near-term growth, and capital markets conditions suggest risky assets such as stocks can continue to do well. That said, the market appears to be entering a more speculative phase, raising the risk of correction.

Performance Review

  • The ClearBridge Appreciation ESG Portfolios underperformed the benchmark S&P 500 Index in the third quarter. On an absolute basis, the Portfolios had gains in seven of 11 sectors. The IT and communication services sectors were the main positive contributors to performance, while the consumer staples and industrials sectors were the main detractors.
  • On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to relative performance during the quarter were ASML (ASML), TJX, Vulcan Materials (VMC), AbbVie (ABBV) and not owning Costco (COST). The biggest detractors were Synopsys, Honeywell International (HON), Emerson Electric, Netflix (NFLX) and an underweight to Apple (AAPL).
  • TJX (TJX), in the consumer discretionary sector, is the leading off-price retailer with over 4,500 stores throughout the U.S., Canada, Europe and Australia primarily under the banner names T.J. Maxx, Marshalls and HomeGoods. The company’s share price rose following strong second-quarter earnings with accelerated same-stores sales and better-than-expected margins. The company also raised its full-year outlook.
  • Vulcan Materials re-affirmed its guidance, noting data center projects require infrastructure spending beneficial to aggregates and Vulcan has a strategically placed footprint. Vulcan’s defensiveness and pricing power also make it attractive amid a slower industrial economy.
  • Synopsys (SNPS), in the IT sector, is a leading provider of electronic design automation software and services to the semiconductor industry. The company’s reset to its earnings expectations, driven by weakness in its IP business, hurt shares and was a thesis changer relative to our previous view, that, given demand trends in the semiconductor industry, Synopsis was a relatively low-risk compounder capitalizing on the secular trend of increasing chip demand. Given the lack of visibility and the potential for continued weakness from IP customers in China and U.S., we exited our position.
  • Emerson Electric (EMR) has performed well following the announcement of “Liberation Day” tariffs in April; investors harvested some gains late in the third quarter.
  • The Portfolios are underweight Apple, which performed well on better-than-expected demand for the iPhone 7.

Outlook

  • As in the second quarter, policy remains supportive of near-term growth, and capital market conditions suggest risky assets such as stocks can continue to do well. That said, the market appears to be entering a more speculative phase, raising the risk of correction. In our view, capital markets will determine whether equities can sustain their advance. If investors keep rewarding AI megaprojects with higher share prices, the cycle can persist. But if markets tire of new multiyear infrastructure commitments or funding AI firms at ever-richer valuations, there is little valuation support to fall back on.
  • We are long-term investors focused on through-the-cycle outperformance via downside protection, and we increasingly believe risks may be starting to outweigh the potential rewards. Accordingly, we keep a close eye on balance sheet and cash flow durability across our holdings to ensure our exposure centers on companies with the financial wherewithal to weather a potential cooling in today’s white-hot environment.

data.title: Avino Silver And Gold Mines: Growth Glitters, But Price Sparkles Too Much (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since I last covered Avino Silver and Gold Mines Ltd. (ASM) with a Buy rating, shares are up 75.07% versus 16.03% for the S&P 500, driven by strong moves in both gold and silver.

Back then, what I particularly liked—and still do—is that ASM is pursuing an organic growth strategy in an M&A-driven world. That approach remains ASM's focus today, and it is clearly paying off—evidenced by progress at La Preciosa and ongoing exploration upside. However, I think much of this recent success and future growth is already priced in, and in this update, I will walk through Q3 results, recent production trends, and growth catalysts—while weighing the risks and valuation and ultimately explaining why I am moving my rating from Buy to Hold.

Q3 Results

I think Q3 results felt like a real inflection point for Avino as they posted record profitability in a really strong precious metals tape and quietly bought out the remaining royalties and contingent payments on the La Preciosa project—giving themselves full ownership.

Revenues came in at $21 million, up 44% year-over-year, and Avino posted its highest-ever quarterly net income of $7.7 million. CF also remained robust, with $57 million in cash and $51 million in working capital, giving the management team plenty of flexibility as they scale up.

I think management also did well to contain costs—with year-to-date AISC coming in essentially flat over the prior comparative period.

In the earnings call, management noted that operations at the Avino mill continue to run well and that development at La Preciosa is moving ahead faster than expected—as material from La Preciosa is already being processed at the Avino mill.

Drilling at La Gloria and Abundancia also continues to deliver high-grade intercepts, indicating to me that there is still much upside left in the resources being explored.

Taken together, I think this is a strong signal that Avino is moving beyond being a one-asset junior and is now nudging toward becoming a credible mid-tier producer.

Recent Production Trends

In my prior article, I had mentioned that Avino had just replaced the jaw crusher at the Avino mill in Q1'25. This necessary upgrade came in handy over the last two quarters as Avino entered a lower-grade sequence in mid-2025 and has had to run more material through the mill to achieve stable production levels. The higher-grade material being trucked in from La Preciosa has also supplemented material from the Avino mine.

Something that investors need to be on the lookout for is the updated Mineral Reserve Estimate that ASM will release soon (Q1'26), as this will likely be an important catalyst for valuation and long-term production planning at La Preciosa.

Valuation

I have selected the following peers for ASM because they strike a balance between smaller and mid-tier silver/gold producers with operations concentrated in the Americas.

I prefer using FWD EV/EBITDA ratios when valuing mining companies, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate.

Over the past two years, ASM traded at an FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 12% above peers, on average. However, ASM is now trading at a ratio that is a staggering 132% (or 1.8 standard deviations) above peers.

Avino is clearly no longer being valued just for its current production. Instead, the market is pricing in the growth that lies ahead—with the company targeting to nearly quadruple production by 2029. Beyond La Preciosa, which should start to meaningfully contribute to this year's production, ASM is also exploring an Oxide Tailings project targeted for 2028. Avino still holds significant legacy waste from its 1980s-1990s operations—material that isn't ideal for the current mill but could become a cost-effective source of production thanks to advances in heap leach technology.

The Technical Take

Looking at the technicals, ASM shares look like they are stalling a bit here. After a strong rally, the stock is still comfortably above the 50-day and 200-day moving averages, but the MACD is showing a bearish divergence, and the RSI is hovering around 50. Personally, I am not seeing this as an ideal entry point. I would be looking for a pullback below $5, roughly 20% or more below current levels, to get more comfortable entering a trade. Of course, if gold and silver continue to rally, the stock might not see $5 again—but I am fine with missing it then rather than chasing it here.

Risks

I think the biggest risk for Avino remains execution. La Preciosa is shaping up to be transformative, but bringing a second mine online is complex and leaves the company open to operational execution challenges. On the regulatory front, future projects like the Oxide Tailings project will need permitting and approvals typical of any cyanide leach facility—which can introduce timing and cost uncertainty even after ASM delivered a strong pre-feasibility study. Given ASM's current status as a small-cap company—albeit one that is quickly transforming to a mid-tier producer—share price volatility may also be a bigger concern than for some of the more established producers.

Takeaway

I am downgrading ASM from a Buy to a Hold. The stock has had an impressive run, and the company has undoubtedly executed well from an operational standpoint. However, I think shares are priced for perfection here and don't leave much room for safety. That said, I remain bullish on gold and silver, and a rising tide will lift all boats. For investors already holding ASM, I think it makes sense to continue backing the company's execution, but I think new buyers should wait for a more attractive entry point or pullback—as I think there are better risk/reward opportunities out there.

data.title: Martin Currie Emerging Markets Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The MSCI Emerging Markets Index was up 10.6% in the third quarter, outpacing developed markets. One of the strongest regions was China, which rose 20.7%, spurred by excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) opportunities and increasing investor interest due to the market’s valuation. Taiwan advanced 14.3% and Korea climbed 12.7%. India was the weakest of the major EM markets, down 7.6% mainly due to a rotation into other regions as well as a subdued consumer backdrop.
  • Contributors: Delta Electronics (DLEGF), CATL, Tencent (TCEHY), Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), Alibaba (BABA) and Antofagasta (ANFGF).
  • Detractors: HDFC Bank (HDB), ICICI Bank (IBN), China Merchants Bank (CIHHF)(CIHKY), Titan, Reliance Industries and MercadoLibre (MELI).
  • Outlook: Looking ahead, we are particularly excited by the powerful synergy of technology adoption, urbanization, and services sector growth prevalent in emerging markets.

Performance Review

  • Several Chinese holdings were leading contributors in the quarter. CATL benefited from improving investor sentiment in China and its leadership role as a battery supplier, with electric vehicle and energy storage system demand strong. Tencent delivered strong operating results and the positive sentiment towards China has benefited the company as the largest Chinese name in the index. Alibaba continues to attract interest due to its leading cloud and AI capabilities, and the positive sentiment change towards the China market. The company announced an increase in AI investment and a new LLM.
  • Other leading contributors included Taiwan’s Delta Electronics, a supplier of power and cooling systems boosted by its current and forecasted leading market share in data center buildouts. Its AI exposure has led to significant earnings upgrades as the market has started to quantify the demand for its products. Korean memory supplier Samsung benefitted from the strong demand for memory, given the intensity of demand for memory in AI applications. Additionally, supply is tight in conventional DRAM. Copper miner Antofagasta, which operates in Chile, was higher as supplies tightened due to a mine shutdown by a competitor. Copper continues to be a beneficiary of the electrification demand.
  • On the downside were a number of Indian holdings, including HDFC and ICICI, which failed to keep pace due to weak Indian market performance. HDFC has broadly been delivering in line with expectations, with an expectation that growth can reaccelerate having improved its deposit base. ICICI has been delivering solid operational fundamentals but has suffered a valuation de-rating. Titan delivered strong growth in its quarterly results but a soft consumer backdrop in India and higher gold prices had some impact on demand. Titan continues to outperform other Indian consumer businesses in terms of growth. Multi-industry conglomerate Reliance Industries has been pressured by weak sentiment towards the Indian market. The market is awaiting potential upcoming IPOs.
  • Outside of India, the primary detractors were China Merchants Bank, which suffered from a rotation out of financials as well as the still tepid Chinese economy; and MercadoLibre, which has faced growing pressures in its key markets of Argentina and Brazil.

Outlook

  • We firmly believe that the long-term investment outlook for EM remains robust and we maintain strong confidence in our portfolio holdings. The market persistently undervalues high-quality, sustainable growth companies and we are convinced that despite the recent, prolonged style rotation, investing in these companies will yield positive results in the long term.
  • Looking ahead, we are particularly excited by the powerful synergy of technology adoption, urbanization, and services sector growth prevalent in EM. We anticipate that our highly selective, stock-focused strategy will thrive by identifying companies with high returns on equity, operating in structurally growing sectors. Furthermore, the current environment—with its regulatory pressures, human conflicts, and ever-evolving macroeconomic landscape—highlights the importance of focusing on strong sustainability and governance characteristics, and sustainable growth as critical factors in determining companies that will remain relevant and lead the way in the long run.
  • As we progress toward 2026, we think the key drivers of EM returns, and thus the biggest opportunities, will be technology, India, and China. Technology companies in EM are core to the global adoption of AI, and the longer-term structural growth opportunity of EM information technology is broader across the whole ecosystem. This should place them well to succeed in the next 12 months and beyond. Similarly, India has had an interesting 2024 and we think that as the market returns to recognizing fundamentals in share prices, there may be a market correction that benefits high-quality, sustainable growth companies. Finally, as the largest country in the asset class, China will continue to play a key role and we believe its recovery has only just begun.

Top Equity Issuers (% of Total)

Holding

Fund

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co Ltd

13.00

data.title: Micron: The Memory Chip Cycle Is Turning Against You
data.description:
Micron Technology (MU) has delivered one of its strongest quarterly results in history, driven by exceptional growth in AI-related demand, record DRAM and NAND prices, and accelerating adoption of high-speed memory. The results and guidance for the first fiscal quarter of 2026 also indicate that we’re currently in the middle of a memory supercycle, which helped margins and profits to reach levels well above the previous peaks.

However, in our view, the stock currently reflects a near-perfect operating environment that is unlikely to persist. Micron is operating at peak cycle profitability, peak prices, and peak utilization, while also seeking a significantly higher capital expenditure cycle. Given that expectations extrapolate current conditions far into the future, we believe the risk-reward profile has become asymmetric. At current levels, Micron shares appear to be priced in for a prolonged supply shortage and continued AI-driven demand growth, leaving limited downside protection if conditions normalize. Because of that, we maintain our Sell rating.

Q1 Was Solid

Micron's latest Q1 earnings results were unquestionably strong. Revenue reached a record $13.6 billion, up 57% year-over-year. DRAM revenue grew 69% year-over-year, while NAND revenue grew 22% year-over-year, driven by tight industry supply and aggressive pricing. Consolidated gross margin increased to 56.8%, up 11% year-over-year, with management targeting a gross margin of approximately 68% in the fiscal second quarter, which would be a historic high for the company.

All of this reflects a very favorable picture for Micron. After all, the demand for AI-powered data centers grew faster than industry capacity expansion, leading to sharp price increases for both DRAM and NAND. At the same time, Micron benefited from this favorable combination, as HBM and data center products have significantly higher margins than legacy consumer memory. As a result, operating income reached $6.4 billion, and free cash flow set a new quarterly record.

While these results support Micron's operating performance, they also highlight how current profitability is subject to extreme pricing conditions. Memory remains a cyclical commodity business, and margins at these levels have historically not been sustainable once supply begins to respond.

Aggressive CapEx Signals Cycle Risks

One of the most important takeaways from the latest conference call was management's decision to increase capital expenditures in fiscal 2026 to approximately $20 billion, up from a previous estimate of $18 billion. The increase is primarily driven by investments in HBM capacity, advanced DRAM nodes, and additional cleanrooms around the world where Micron has a presence. While management has emphasized capital discipline, this increase in spending marks a clear shift to an expansion phase.

This could become a major issue for Micron in the long run. Historically, memory capacity cycles have tended to drive capacity expansion across the industry, which ultimately compresses the impact of pricing. While short-term supply constraints remain real, the delayed impact of increased capital spending often results in oversupply once new capacity is brought online. Moreover, HBM production directly competes with traditional DRAM capacity due to shared wafer resources. As Micron and its competitors allocate more capacity to HBM, any slowdown in AI demand could lead to an industry oversupply and limited flexibility. In our view, the current trajectory of capital spending is increasing earnings volatility instead of reducing it.

AI Demand Concentration Increases Downside Risk

Management has repeatedly emphasized that AI-related demand is driving much of the incremental growth, particularly in the high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and data center segments. HBM supply for 2026 is already fully committed under multi-year agreements, and Micron expects the overall addressable HBM market to grow by around 40% on average through 2028. While this narrative supports short-term visibility, it also increases concentration risk. Much of Micron's growth is driven by a relatively small group of hyperscale customers and AI accelerator platforms. Any mitigation of AI infrastructure costs, changes in architectural efficiency, or customer resource repurposing could have an outsized impact on pricing and usage.

For the second quarter of fiscal 2026, Micron is forecasting revenue of approximately $18.7 billion and EPS of $8.42, well above previous consensus expectations. These numbers suggest a rapid continuation of the pricing trend and margin expansion. However, this forecast also significantly raises the bar for the coming quarters. At current valuation levels, investors appear to be pricing in sustained peak margin performance that will continue beyond fiscal 2026. This assumption ignores the inherent cyclicality of memory markets and the historical tendency for prices to change as supply increases. Even a slight slowdown in AI server deployments or normalization in DRAM and NAND prices could lead to a sharp earnings revision.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

There are scenarios in which Micron could continue to outperform and undermine our bearish thesis. Demand driven by AI could remain stronger and more resilient than in previous technology cycles, particularly if memory per system continues to grow. Multi-year supply agreements could also provide more stable earnings than in previous cycles, while Micron's technology leadership could allow it to capture a disproportionate share of industry profits.

In addition, if capacity expansion remains constrained by geopolitical, regulatory, or enforcement issues, higher prices could persist longer than expected. These factors could delay a typical cyclical downturn.

The Real Value of Micron

Since our last coverage three months ago, Micron has delivered a notably strong quarterly performance, prompting us to update our revenue forecasts and revise the key assumptions underlying our financial model.

Despite this near-term improvement, we continue to view Micron as a higher-risk investment at current levels. The stock trades at an elevated valuation relative to its cash flow profile, while operating margins remain among the weakest within the semiconductor peer group. At the same time, the company is entering a prolonged period of elevated capital spending, which we believe will constrain free cash flow generation and limit upside for shareholders.

Our updated valuation model was constructed when Micron’s stock was trading at $343.43 per share. We applied a 3% perpetual growth rate, consistent with the methodology we typically use across semiconductor valuations. The projection spans the next five years, incorporating cash and total debt figures from the latest earnings report.

The model uses a discount rate of 9.39%, derived from the cost of equity and cost of debt. The cost of debt was calculated using the latest report results, while the cost of equity was estimated through CAPM, applying a risk-free rate of 4.14%, a beta of 1.50, and a market return of 7.77%.

Our sales forecast is similar to the current consensus estimates. We assume that the operating margin will stabilize around 30% by the end of fiscal 2026. The tax rate is set at 12%, consistent with management’s latest guidance. Capital expenditure is projected at approximately $20 billion for FY2026, matching the most recent management guidance. In addition, the changes in net working capital are modeled at 1% of revenue, which is in line with our previous valuation on Micron.

Based on these assumptions, our model estimates Micron’s enterprise value at $322.23 billion. Adjusting for cash and debt, we calculated an equity value, which is $320.05 billion. On a per-share basis, this implies an intrinsic value of $284.50, which gives us a downside from the current market price of around 17%.

Final Thoughts

Micron is delivering exceptionally strong results in a favorable environment. Record revenue, margin growth, and strong guidance underscore the company's operational strength and relevance in building AI-powered data centers.

However, these results also reflect peak-cycle conditions supported by tight supply, aggressive pricing, and accelerating capital spending. Given expectations that extrapolate current momentum far into the future, we believe the stock offers limited upside. Rising capital spending, concentrated AI demand, and the historical cyclicality of memory markets create an environment where downside risks outweigh incremental upside. At current levels, we view Micron as ideally priced and reaffirm our Sell rating.
